title: Listening - Part 3: Is the Internet Killing Language?
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.
instructions: You will hear an interview with a linguist, Noah, and a social media historian, Sonia, about how the internet is changing the way we speak and write. For questions 15–20, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which fits best.

extracts.context: An interview exploring 'internet slang,' the role of emojis as 'digital gestures,' and whether formal grammar is still relevant.
extracts.audio_script:
Interviewer: LOL, BRB, and emojis—is the internet ruining the English language or making it more vibrant? Joining me are Dr. Noah, a linguist, and Sonia, who studies the history of digital culture. Noah, are you worried about the decline of proper grammar?

Noah: [15] Not at all. Language is a living thing; if it isn't changing, it’s dying. What we’re seeing isn't a 'decline' but 'code-switching.' [/15] Most young people know how to write a formal essay, but they also know that using a full stop at the end of a WhatsApp message can make them look angry or aggressive. They are learning new social cues that didn't exist twenty years ago.

Interviewer: Sonia, emojis are everywhere. Are they a step backward toward hieroglyphics?

Sonia: [16] I see them as 'digital gestures.' When we speak in person, we use our hands and our facial expressions to provide context. In text, that's impossible. [/16] An emoji fills that gap. It tells the reader if a sentence is a joke, a flirtation, or a genuine complaint. They aren't replacing words; they are adding the 'tone of voice' that is usually lost in writing.

Interviewer: Noah, what about the speed of language change? New slang seems to appear and disappear in weeks.

Noah: [17] That’s the 'accelerant' effect of social media. In the past, a new word might take decades to travel from London to New York. Now, a meme can change the vocabulary of the entire world in a single afternoon. [/17] But this also means words become 'uncool' much faster. As soon as a brand or a parent starts using a slang term, the younger generation abandons it.

Interviewer: Sonia, is the internet making our language more global or more divided?

Sonia: [18] Both. We have a 'Global English' that everyone understands, but we also see the rise of 'micro-dialects'—communities on specific platforms like Reddit or TikTok that have their own very specific vocabularies. [/18] It’s a way of signaling that you 'belong' to a certain tribe. If you don't know the slang, you’re an outsider.

Interviewer: Noah, does the autocorrect feature on our phones make us 'lazy' thinkers?

Noah: [19] It changes the *nature* of the task. We spend less time on spelling and more time on the 'flow' of the idea. However, there is a risk that we lose the 'nuance' of vocabulary if we always just accept the first word the phone suggests. [/19]

Interviewer: Final thoughts, Sonia? Will books from 2026 be readable in a hundred years?

Sonia: [20] They’ll be readable, but they might need a 'slang dictionary' to explain the context. The biggest challenge for future historians won't be the words, but the memes and cultural references that provide the subtext. [/20]

Interviewer: Noah, Sonia, thank you.

questions.question: Noah believes that using a full stop in a digital message
options: A) is a sign of a high level of education.
B) helps to clarify the meaning of a sentence.
C) can be interpreted as having a negative tone.
D) is becoming more common among young people.
questions.explanation: He says a full stop on WhatsApp can make someone look 'angry or aggressive'.

questions.question: What is Sonia's view of emojis?
options: A) They are a lazy alternative to using proper vocabulary.
B) They function in a similar way to physical body language.
C) They are confusing for people who didn't grow up with them.
D) They will eventually replace written words entirely.
questions.explanation: She calls them 'digital gestures' that provide context like hands and facial expressions do in person.

questions.question: According to Noah, why does modern slang disappear so quickly?
options: A) Because the words are too difficult to pronounce correctly.
B) Because older people and companies begin to use the terms.
C) Because social media platforms are constantly being deleted.
D) Because young people have very short attention spans.
questions.explanation: He states that as soon as a brand or parent uses a slang term, 'the younger generation abandons it'.

questions.question: Sonia suggests that 'micro-dialects' on the internet are used to
options: A) make it harder for the government to track users.
B) help people practice their translation skills.
C) show that a person is part of a specific online group.
D) prevent people from other countries from reading the posts.
questions.explanation: She says they are a way of 'signaling that you belong to a certain tribe'.

questions.question: Noah is concerned that autocorrect might cause people to
options: A) forget how to write using a physical pen.
B) stop thinking about the subtle differences between words.
C) spend too much time worrying about their spelling.
D) send messages to the wrong person by accident.
questions.explanation: He mentions the risk of losing 'nuance' if we always accept the first word the phone suggests.

questions.question: Sonia predicts that in the future, people will need help understanding
options: A) the basic grammar rules of the early 21st century.
B) how to operate the digital devices used in 2026.
C) the cultural context and humor behind today's writing.
D) why people spent so much time on social media.
questions.explanation: She believes future historians will struggle with the 'memes and cultural references' that provide the subtext.
